analogy: Think of "Alternative Centres of Power" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview In this chapter we shall discuss the role of international organisations after the collapse of the Soviet Union. We shall examine how, in this emerging world, there were calls for the restructuring of international organisations to cope with various new challenges including the... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Alternative Centres of Power is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that o verview In this chapter we shall discuss the role of international organisations after the collapse of the Soviet Union.

The text explains that we shall examine how, in this emerging world, there were calls for the restructuring of international organisations to cope with various new challenges including the rise of US power.

The text explains that the potential reform of the United Nations Security Council is an interesting case of the reform process and its difficulties.

The text explains that we then turn to India’s involvement in the UN and its view of Security Council reforms.

The text explains that the chapter closes by asking if the UN can play any role in dealing with a world dominated by one superpower.

The text explains that in this chapter we also look at some other trans-national organisations that are playing a crucial role.

The text explains that chapter 4 International Organisations This is the United Nations’ logo.

The text explains that the emblem has a world map with olive branches around it, signifying world peace.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 46 Why InternatIonal organIsatIons?

The text explains that both the cartoons comment on the ineffectiveness of the United Nations Organisation, usually referred to as the UN, in the Lebanon crisis in 2006.

The text explains that both the cartoons represent the kind of opinions that we often hear about the UN.

The text explains that on the other hand, we also find that the UN is generally regarded as the most important international organisation in today’s world.

The text explains that in the eyes of many people all over the world, it is indispensable and represents the great hope of humanity for peace and progress.

The text explains that why do we then need organisations like the UN?

The text explains that let us hear two insiders: “The United Nations was not created to take humanity to heaven, but to save it from hell.” — Dag Hammarskjold, the UN’s second Secretary-General. “Talking shop?

The text explains that yes, there are a lot of speeches and meetings at the U.N., especially during the annual sessions of the General Assembly.

The text explains that as temperatures rise because of the increase in greenhouse gases in the atmosphere, there is a danger that sea levels will also rise, thereby submerging many coastal areas of the world including huge cities.

The text explains that member State % 1 USA 22.0 2 China 12.0 3 Japan 8.5 4 Germany 6.0 5 UK 4.5 6 France 4.4 7 Italy 3.3 8 Brazil 2.9 9 Canada 2.7 10 Russia 2.4 11 Republic of Korea 2.2 12 Australia 2.2 13 Spain 2.1 14 Turkey 1.3 15 Netherlands 1.3 16 Mexico 1.2 17 Saudi Arabia 1.1 18 Switzerland 1.1 19 Argentina 0.9 20 Sweden 0.9 21 India 0.8 Source: www.un.org

The text explains that is it possible or even desirable for it to intervene in Source: www.un.org Find out about the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

The text explains that the fact that the UN is physically located within the US territory gives Washington additional sources of influence.

The text explains that resolves disputes between and among member countries f.

The text explains that provides shelter and medical help during emergencies g.

The text explains that administration and coordination of UN affairs i.

The text explains that facilitates free trade among member countries 8.

The text explains that what are the functions of the Security Council?

The text explains that as a citizen of India, how would you support India’s candidature for the permanent membership of the Security Council?

The text explains that critically evaluate the difficulties involved in implementing the suggested reforms to reconstruct the UN.

The text explains that though the UN has failed in preventing wars and related miseries, nations prefer its continuation.

The text explains that what makes the UN an indispensable organisation?

The text explains that 12. ‘Reforming the UN means restructuring of the Security Council’.

Source: NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics, Chapter 4: Alternative Centres of Power

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1941 August: Signing of the Atlantic Charter by the US and 1942 January: 26 Allied nations fighting against the Axis as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Alternative Centres of Power; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Alternative Centres of Power through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1941 August: Signing of the Atlantic Charter by the US and 1942 January: 26 Allied nations fighting against the Axis to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
